ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class; no specific brinjal/eggplant biryani subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biryani — mixed rice dish with vegetables and spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated brinjal biryani entry; A0FKP (biryani) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for brinjal biryani or biryani as a food concept in the clinical terminology.

main_ingredients: Whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon)
Ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of brinjal biryani weighs approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-person serving as listed on GrabFood Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) — Vegetable Biryani and Basmati Rice composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact brinjal biryani entry exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN entries for vegetable biryani and cooked basmati rice, weighted to reflect the brinjal-dominant vegetable component and ghee-based cooking. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for eggplant (ID 169228) and basmati rice (ID 169704). Nutrient values are treated as medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI 57)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (Atkinson et al., 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brinjal biryani. GI of 57 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source: white basmati rice (GI 57, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The vegetable and spice components do not materially alter the GI of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (57 × 85.0) / 100 = 48.45, rounded to 48.7 accounting for fibre adjustment. Available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = (21.25 − 2.0) × 4 = 77.0 g net; GL = (57 × 77.0) / 100 = 43.9 per serving. Note: GL is high (>20) at the 400 g default serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 237.5 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium (~41% of the 2300 mg daily limit), with limited potassium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g per meal, request reduced-salt and reduced-ghee preparation, and avoid adding table salt at the table to keep total sodium intake within the 2300 mg/day threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 21.25 g/100 g with GI 57 (medium); a 400 g serving delivers approximately 85 g total carbohydrate and a glycemic load >40, which is clinically high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict serving size to 150–200 g, pair with a high-fibre low-GI side such as cucumber raita, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high glycemic load at standard serving size poses a significant risk of postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.0 g/100 g from ghee; trans fat 0.13 g/100 g; sodium 237.5 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving contributes 12 g saturated fat and ~950 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200 g, request ghee-reduced or oil-substituted preparation to lower saturated fat intake, and choose this dish only occasionally given the combined saturated fat and sodium burden at standard serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, cumin, ginger-garlic) present; saturated fat below 5 g/100 g threshold; nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomato, chilli) may trigger sensitivity in some individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis can consume brinjal biryani in moderate portions; those with known nightshade sensitivity should monitor for symptom flares and may request preparation without tomato or chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brinjal Biryani is a fragrant North Indian vegetarian rice dish in which tender eggplant (brinjal/aubergine) is marinated in spiced yogurt, sautéed until golden, and then layered with saffron-infused long-grain basmati rice before being slow-cooked using the traditional dum (sealed-pot steam) method. The dish is seasoned with a complex blend of whole spices — including bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon — and ground spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala, finished with fried onions, fresh mint, and coriander leaves. The result is a richly aromatic, mildly spiced rice dish with a soft, slightly smoky eggplant texture contrasting with fluffy, separate grains of basmati rice. Per 100 g it provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 3.75 g protein, 7.0 g fat (3.0 g saturated, primarily from ghee), 21.25 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 237.5 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium, based on basmati rice), but the glycemic load at the standard 400 g restaurant serving is high (>40), making portion control essential for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal and vegetarian. It is commonly served in Indian restaurants and on food-delivery platforms in Singapore as a main course, typically accompanied by raita (yogurt condiment) or a side salad.

confidence_reason: No exact brinjal biryani entry exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani and basmati rice entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for eggplant and basmati rice. The input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7; values are plausible and internally consistent but cannot be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or exact published composite. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for vegetable biryani prepared with ghee are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish are not reported in the adapted ICMR-NIN source; estimated ~180 mg/100 g from vegetable components but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite brinjal biryani dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2026. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.13 g/100 g is retained from the input record; this is plausible given ghee content (naturally occurring ruminant trans fats, primarily vaccenic acid), consistent with USDA data for ghee. 5. Glycemic load per 400 g serving is clinically high (>40); this is flagged in health_context for Type 2 Diabetes. 6. The dish is classified as a Singaporean-served North Indian dish; multilingual names are provided in English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per G6 rules for Singaporean dishes. 7. Input source listed ICMR-NIN URL pointing to dietary guidelines PDF; the nutritive value data are from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods compendium, which is an approved primary regional source.
